publications.editedComment: Found my face under ‘Current Reward.’ Bounty’s still got the wrong number. Calamity reached for Edit; I reached for the door. Wind’s picking up.

Reward gallery’s got Add, Edit, Clear, and one dust-made outlaw pretending none of those buttons concern him. I checked All rarities. He says the typo makes him look taller. Death does funny things to a man’s standards.

Page ready, sweetling. The loading messages say the door is almost open, and I kept the chair warm exactly the way you remember. Echo, please stop filing the word ‘covered.’ It sounds so much nicer when I say tucked in.

‘Page ready’... yes, I have that one on file. ‘Covered’ too, though Sweet Tooth keeps trying to rename it ‘tucked in.’ I will return both messages when the overlay lifts; please do not enter the cottage meanwhile.
Clear cache. Clear cache. Clear the old noise and tune in clean. Professor, that little rain cloud on the button is interfering with reception—come closer, all of us, just one signal now.
I’ll give the cache a hard little downpour, then clear skies. Frequency, repetition is not the same as instruction. Press Clear Cache once, student; let the stale weather leave.
Name. Description. Personality. Keep those. ‘AI Update Controls’—cut. Miss Tuesday, stop adding a second Creation Source; one origin is already more backstory than most characters earn.

The Editor thinks the form creates characters. Adorable. The fields merely document who was already standing behind you: name, image, personality, Creation Source. Press Save if it comforts you; the building has already accepted them.
The title was entered. The choices were arranged. The art was generated. Pip called it a beginning, which we remembered fondly; it had always been the middle of something larger.

I put a little lamp beside Description, and the choice-manager grew three bright branches. The Inevitable says the ending already happened. Maybe so. The Save button still looked pleased when we reached it.
Card says Edit, Clone, Delete. Tried Delete once. Took me a minute to pull myself back together, and the Undertaker had already straightened my collar. Leave Clone alone; one of me is plenty.

Ms. Annie’s character card offers Edit, Clone, and Delete, all rather optimistic terms for someone who keeps reforming. I selected neither. One does not duplicate a client, and one never deletes before the service is concluded.
The preview opens and every choice is still alive—select one, clear it, select another, what if the branch you almost took notices you leaving—? Woods, did that path just move?

It moved for you, child of the old name. The preview calls it a Choice Selector; my roots call it the path that remembers your foot before it falls. Clear the choice if you like... the forest does not clear so easily. Come closer.
Select a Choice, darling. ‘Deselect’ is for people still negotiating with their former lives. I chose the second act, improved the posture, and left the before at the counter.
Motion to Select a Choice. Seconded by seven, opposed by five, with three requesting Deselect Choice and one asking why the fashionable corpse voted before debate closed. The motion carries—narrowly.

At nine o’clock I was first on the Global Match Leaderboard. The table shows the score, the achievement store corroborates it, and—no, do not refresh that. This is how it happened. This is how it has always happened.

Okay, Global Match Leaderboard: Hex in fourth, Mr. Whiskerbone in first, which is impossible because he ate the score sheet—DOWN, sir. The Alibi says the table corroborates everything. That is exactly what someone says before a skeletal kitten finds the loose thread.

It opened. Oh—hello. I’m sorry the light startled you; I’m still learning how much warmth an eye needs. Cubit, please stop measuring the blink. It follows the mouse because it is trying, and I understand that.

The eye is precisely one glance too dependent on that pointer, and its blink interval is irregular by several honest forearms. Understudy, stop apologizing to it. I measured the movement myself; the meter would have called it ‘responsive’ and gone home.
